title: Partition Equal Subset Sum
code: foreach (int x in nums) total += x;
for (int s = target; s >= x; s--)

dp.label: dp[s] = is sum s reachable by some subset so far?  (index = s, 0..target)

frames.caption: total = 22 is even, so target = total/2 = 11. Base case: dp[0] = true (empty subset), all other dp[s] = false.
frames.note: The state is dp[s] = can we reach sum s with some subset of the items processed so far? Only dp[0] starts true — the empty subset sums to 0 — and every other entry is false because, before seeing any item, no positive sum is reachable. We will turn cells true as items unlock new sums.

frames.caption: Item x=1: sweep s from target=11 DOWN to 1, setting dp[s] |= dp[s-1].
frames.note: The sweep runs s from target DOWN to x, never up. Going downward, when we read dp[s-x] it still reflects the table from BEFORE this item — so x is counted at most once (0/1 knapsack). Ascending would let the freshly-set dp[s-x] feed dp[s] again, reusing x and solving the wrong, unbounded problem. This loop direction is the entire trick.

frames.caption: s=1: dp[0] = T, so adding 1 reaches 1. Read dp[1-1] = dp[0].
frames.note: To decide if sum s becomes reachable by ADDING item x, we ask a strictly smaller subproblem: was s − x already reachable WITHOUT x? That is dp[s-x]. If yes, then "that earlier subset plus x" reaches s, so dp[s] becomes true. We are reading dp[s-x] and writing dp[s] — the classic knapsack transition dp[s] |= dp[s-x].

frames.caption: Set dp[1] = true — 1 is now a reachable subset sum.
frames.note: dp[s-x] was true, so a subset summing to s−x exists; dropping x onto it reaches s. dp[s] flips to true and stays a candidate for every later item. This is the only way a false cell becomes true — it is exactly one new reachable sum recorded.

frames.caption: Item x=5: sweep s from target=11 DOWN to 5, setting dp[s] |= dp[s-5].
frames.note: The sweep runs s from target DOWN to x, never up. Going downward, when we read dp[s-x] it still reflects the table from BEFORE this item — so x is counted at most once (0/1 knapsack). Ascending would let the freshly-set dp[s-x] feed dp[s] again, reusing x and solving the wrong, unbounded problem. This loop direction is the entire trick.

frames.caption: s=6: dp[1] = T, so adding 5 reaches 6. Read dp[6-5] = dp[1].
frames.note: To decide if sum s becomes reachable by ADDING item x, we ask a strictly smaller subproblem: was s − x already reachable WITHOUT x? That is dp[s-x]. If yes, then "that earlier subset plus x" reaches s, so dp[s] becomes true. We are reading dp[s-x] and writing dp[s] — the classic knapsack transition dp[s] |= dp[s-x].

frames.caption: Set dp[6] = true — 6 is now a reachable subset sum.
frames.note: dp[s-x] was true, so a subset summing to s−x exists; dropping x onto it reaches s. dp[s] flips to true and stays a candidate for every later item. This is the only way a false cell becomes true — it is exactly one new reachable sum recorded.

frames.note: To decide if sum s becomes reachable by ADDING item x, we ask a strictly smaller subproblem: was s − x already reachable WITHOUT x? That is dp[s-x]. If yes, then "that earlier subset plus x" reaches s, so dp[s] becomes true. We are reading dp[s-x] and writing dp[s] — the classic knapsack transition dp[s] |= dp[s-x].

frames.caption: Return dp[11] = T. Sum 11 is reachable (subset {11}, mirrored by {1,5,5}) → the array CAN be split into two equal halves.
frames.note: dp[target] true means some subset sums to total/2, so its complement sums to total/2 too — an equal partition. The cost is O(n × target) = O(n × sum/2) time and O(target) space. Name it as PSEUDO-POLYNOMIAL: polynomial in the numeric value of the sum, not the input bit-length, so a huge target can still be slow.
